narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63.2% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 25.50, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 2 and 1 respectively, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on the strongest influences for your specific profile. The most decisive factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you lower than typical among those who passed (where the average is around the 6th decile), signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance. Next in influence, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, as its solid level pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing compared to lower scores that hurt others. Your LSAT score had a smaller but negative pull, contributing toward failure since it falls well below the average of 37.27 seen in students who succeeded on the bar. Overall, these top three elements—dominated by your later-year grades—outweighed positives like your GPA and aligned more closely with profiles of students who struggled on the exam. For context, successful applicants typically show higher decile rankings throughout undergrad and grad years, stronger entrance exam results, and often come from upper-middle or high-income backgrounds, though your middle-income status and full-time commitment are common and neutral here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven forecast based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth. Many students in similar positions improve these metrics and succeed on reapplication—reach out to our admissions office for personalized advice on building a stronger case next cycle. With focused effort, you can shift these predictors in your favor.
